Case Narrative

In brief, for the investigating officer:

  • DEEPA CHAUHAN spread money to 289 different accounts.
  • DEEPA CHAUHAN collected money from 111 different accounts.
  • Rs 3,863,449.00 of incoming funds was traced to onward transfers or withdrawals.
  • The account to investigate first is YASH DUBEY, which shows the strongest combination of suspicious behaviors in this case.

4 · Findings by Account

YASH DUBEY strong
Account Number 25078124219247 · DCB Bank · Score 3,832
Rank #1

This account is suspicious due to an extreme credit of 400000.00, which was subsequently allocated into 17 debit transactions. The account also exhibits unusual patterns, including round-value debits and a complex money flow graph. These behaviors warrant further investigation.

  • Money Trail Tracing strong — A credit of 400000.00 was traced into 17 subsequent debit transactions, with the entire amount allocated and none remaining untraced. (+36 more of this type)
  • Duplicate Verification weak — Two transactions, ref:681310110010629, shared an account, direction, near-equal amount, and nearby date, indicating potential duplication. (+2 more of this type)
  • Money Flow Graph Construction structural — A money-flow graph with 540 nodes and 919 directed transaction edges was constructed, indicating a complex transaction network.
  • Round Value Debit Patterns weak — The account made 7 suspicious round-value debits totalling 267000.00, which are multiples of 1000.

Data Security & Method (Appendix)

What stays on this machine

  • All fraud detection, scoring and ranking run on this machine (deterministic code).
  • Raw bank statements and extracted transactions never leave the machine.
  • The money-flow graph, clusters and suspicion scores are computed locally.

What is sent to an external service

  • Only small, redacted text samples are sent to the language model to describe a statement's column layout when local parsing is insufficient.
  • For images / scanned PDFs, the page pixels are sent to a vision model (the one unavoidable case).
  • The language model only rewrites explanations into plain language; it never changes a number, account, or decision.
